attributes.title: Sonio - escalation policy not paging
attributes.summary:
An escalation policy triggered through a workflow says it paged in workflow logs but the actual responders did not get paged.
Alert: https://rootly.com/account/alerts/4WzZay
Workflow Run: https://rootly.com/account/runs/488d442b-4e0a-4807-85f5-4f2288a8d0d5
Escalation Policy: https://rootly.com/account/escalation-policies/6eebf2be-9973-45bc-92a5-fc2eabfc498f/edit

attributes.title: Incorrect Paging Following EP Config- XPO
attributes.summary:
https://rootly.com/account/alerts/ZjPAql?tab=timeline
Team ID: 3732 (XPO)
Issues with this alert:
1. Initial Page to EP https://rootly.com/account/escalation-policies/590f3b73-d2fd-410d-a10d-a096988d83d5/edit UI shows that each notification was sent 3 times (Twilio shows one call)
2. Customer claims alert was ack'd on the initial phone calls from the responder (alert did not ack)
 3. User's notifications are set to go off 2 minutes then 3 minutes after initial page, escalation policy is set to go off 5 minutes after initial page. The next notifications were 14 minutes after initial page. 
4. The escalation policy then hit level 2 and level 3 simultaneously (level 3 set for 5 minutes after level 2)
5. Alert was then ack'd by level 2 responder via mobile and level 1 responder via phone call

Why was the escalation policy and user notification times not properly respected? 
Are there any logs around the ack on the initial phone call for level 1? 
Why were 3 sets of notifications sent on the initial triggering of notifications for this alert?

attributes.title: Outsystem Scheduling issues (disappearing shifts)
attributes.summary: OutSystems has missing shifts from their schedules despite rotations being set up correctly.

attributes.title: Redis | Engine CPU High - on-call-scheduler-production-redis-001 [PRODUCTION]
attributes.summary:
Impact:

1/ over the last 1.5 hours we had brief periods where the scheduler redis was pegged. longest period was 14 mins. During that time, paging for the first level of an EP worked no problem, but subsequent levels could have been delayed up to the 14 min mark. We did not miss any escalations, they just might have been delayed. 

2/ alerting events queue was slow to process, which means that timeline events could be slow to show up in the UI.

https://rootly.com/account/alerts/bAvbxl

https://us-east-1.console.aws.amazon.com/elasticache/home?region=us-east-1#/redis/on-call-scheduler-production-redis

attributes.title: Trivago User Group SCIM Assignments
attributes.summary:
Trivago's SCIM group assignments are mapping to observer over a custom role. Trivago has incident permission sets enabled, we need to determine if the permissions in the incident permission sets are properly being taken into account when determining which role to assign. 

Team ID: 2519

attributes.title: Alerts incorrectly deduping
attributes.summary:
Alerts are incorrectly being deduplicated against resolved alerts.

Customer Impact: Critical reliability loss for customer (Pylon ticket #19005). This is the second occurrence of this issue and affects their MTTD & MTTR during high-throughput holiday period.

Immediate Mitigation: Manually modified the affected alert to prevent further deduplication.

Theory: When Grafana resolved the alert and a user acknowledged it within ~1 second, the events may have been processed out of order across internal systems, leaving deduplication state inconsistent with the actual alert status shown in the UI.

attributes.title: Alert Source stopped working for customer
attributes.summary:
Alertmanager webhook issue for customer (mimir source: 2dc5b798-2384-478b-8e4b-49c49d5e0212)
Webhooks stopped creating alerts ~15:02 UTC. Rails DB shows source is healthy, webhooks were processing fine before that. Customer curl tests return {"action":"trigger_async","ids":[]} (accepted but empty) - a duplicate alert source with same payload works fine.
Response format indicates this is going through Notification Service, not direct Rails controller. Need someone to check NS logs for why this source specifically is returning empty ids. Customer made some alert route changes around the same time (service removed then re-added) which may have corrupted state.
Request IDs from customer curl tests to search in Datadog:
0487f107-15f6-4745-af65-77c8fe130c1f
5301675e-f5a5-48e1-ab48-6d546701008b

attributes.title: Shift Gaps In Three Amo Schedules
attributes.summary:
Amo has 4 schedules that currently have no users on Call when there should be on-call users. All 3 three schedules have gaps that begin at the same time: 3 AM EST. 

1. sugar-paging-schedule - f99eef7e-3c03-4eea-be13-a4e29cebcefa
2. growth-paging-schedule - d8ec1459-1218-42f9-b1ff-760c549a79e4
3. bump-paging-schedule - 17f624a4-f3e3-4124-846a-5f7a09e0d500
4. sre-paging-schedule - 4173f99f-97b1-47a1-9101-097edad9b60b

Amo is using Terraform ( pulumi ) to manage their schedules.

attributes.summary: https://rootly.sentry.io/issues/7113844156/?alert_rule_id=16230237&alert_type=issue&notification_uuid=cb4b3034-73d8-4ab1-9319-dbea8888d460&project=4509852680388608&referrer=slack

executive_summary.primary_concern: 1 team member(s) showing moderate OCB burnout warning signs (25-74/100)

insights.llm_team_analysis:
## Summary

The concentration of incident response work reveals a troubling imbalance: just 18 people shoulder the entire on-call burden for an 84-person engineering organization, creating a response-to-team ratio that leaves most engineers insulated from production reality while a small subset absorbs operational stress. Alex Thompson's OCB score of 76.5 signals severe burnout territory after handling 10 incidents this month, crossing into the critical intervention threshold. Weekend work touches 6 engineers despite relatively modest after-hours commit patterns (8.7% late nights, 4.0% weekends across 298 commits), suggesting the operational load rather than development work drives the concerning patterns. With 66 members carrying zero incident responsibility, this team has inadvertently created a two-tier system where operational knowledge and stress concentrate dangerously.

## Key Observations

• **Alex Thompson requires immediate manager intervention** – with an OCB score of 76.5 placing him in the severe burnout range and 10 incidents handled, he's absorbed more than half the incident load of any individual responder while the median team member handles zero, indicating he's become a single point of failure for tribal knowledge and incident resolution.

• **The 21.4% active responder rate creates institutional fragility** – when 78.6% of the engineering team never touches production incidents, you're building a knowledge moat where operational expertise can't diffuse, making it nearly impossible to scale on-call coverage or provide relief to the 18 people who actually respond when systems break.

• **Weekend work patterns don't align with commit volume intensity** – only 4.0% of commits happen on weekends yet 6 engineers are working those days, suggesting they're handling incident follow-up, infrastructure maintenance, or synchronous production support rather than planned feature development, which represents hidden operational toil not captured in development velocity metrics.

positive_indicators: Majority of team showing healthy stress levels
